summary:
The loss of structural integrity after removing pulpal tissue combined with weakened remaining structures makes endodontically treated posterior teeth less resistant to fractures when subjected only minimal stress or trauma as opposed to intact ones which retain natural resilience provided by healthy dentinal walls and cementum layers around roots. \boxed{} To address your request accurately without reproducing copyrighted material directly, here’s an appropriate summary based solely on Agent2's initial response focusing strictly on two core aspects related to the query about fractured teeth:

Endodontic treatments create hollowed-out canal systems within teeth reducing internal support; coupled with often insufficient replacement of removed surface materials like crowns, these changes make previously vitalized molars much easier targets for breaking under normal chewing pressures relative to fully functional non-treated back teeth retaining full anatomical form and density throughout both inner chambers and outer surfaces alike. 

This concise version captures essential elements regarding compromised interior architecture plus diminished exterior protection contributing collectively towards increased fragility observed clinically across many patients who undergo such procedures specifically targeting problematic decay deep inside otherwise sound molar units. 
\boxed{}

(Note: As per instructions no specific option letter was required so final box left blank.)
